hero.name: Sambhav Lamichhane
hero.tagline: I build at the intersection of finance, data, and technology.
hero.subSubline: Senior at University of St. Thomas · Graduating May 2026 · From Kathmandu, Nepal

sections.title: My Story
sections.body: I grew up in Kathmandu, Nepal, with the Himalayas as my backdrop and a family motorcycle dealership as my first business classroom. That ground-level education in how money actually moves shaped everything I've built since. I came to the US for college, won First-Year Class President in my first month knowing no one, and have spent the last three years building at the intersection of markets and technology: from algorithmic trading research to co-founding a fintech platform for unbanked workers to managing a fleet of 100+ VR headsets. I've bungee jumped off a 750-foot bridge in Nepal and walked an 80-year-old through sending her first email. Both were equally rewarding.

bullets: First international student elected Class President at UST
$6,000 won across business competitions with Ruta'al
75+ backtests built as Quantitative Trading Researcher

stats.label: International student Class President at UST

journey.title: Kathmandu roots
journey.impact: Grew up in Kathmandu with the Himalayas as my backdrop. My first business classroom was a family motorcycle dealership where I watched money move, margins compress, and customers negotiate. Learned early that numbers tell stories, if you know how to read them.

journey.title: The family showroom
journey.impact: Worked the floor at our Bajaj motorcycle dealership in Kathmandu, demoing products, handling objections, closing sales, and following up with customers after the fact. Also helped reconcile accounts and tighten financial controls, cutting discrepancies by 15%. First lesson in the gap between what a product does and how you sell it.

journey.title: Crossing an ocean
journey.impact: Moved to Minnesota knowing no one. In my first month, I ran for First-Year Class President carrying a homemade poster on a yardstick, talking to students one by one. Won. Became the first international student elected to the role at UST. Managed a $115,000 budget across 150+ student organizations. Learned that showing up differently is a strategy.

journey.title: London and the library
journey.impact: Studied abroad in London. Volunteered at Kilburn Library in Camden teaching digital literacy to 75+ residents and 50+ local entrepreneurs. Built permanent instructional guides so the help would outlast my presence. An 80-year-old sent her first email. I've bungee jumped off a 750-foot bridge in Nepal; that felt about equal.

journey.title: Building in three places at once
journey.impact: Managing the VR Lab at STELAR, a fleet of 100+ Meta Quest headsets serving 500+ students across nursing and business programs. Simultaneously running 75+ algorithmic trading backtests as a Quantitative Researcher. Joined LEAF Lab as a Product Management Fellow, working across EdTech and eCommerce products in agile sprints. Shipped CardSense and VibeTicker on the side using Cursor and Gemini API. Learned what it actually means to have too many tabs open, and how to prioritize anyway.

journey.title: Ruta'al and what's next
journey.impact: Co-founding a USSD-based fintech platform serving unbanked informal workers in Mexico and Nepal, places where financial infrastructure is a privilege, not a given. Won $6,000 across business competitions. Building the product, running investor pitches, and finishing my degree simultaneously. Graduating May 2026. Looking for the next hard problem.

projects.name: Ruta'al: Financial Inclusion Platform
projects.description: Co-founded a USSD-based fintech platform serving unbanked informal workers in Mexico and Nepal. Built the functional prototype, conducted TAM/SAM/SOM analysis, led investor pitches, and won $6,000 across business competitions.
projects.projectContext: USSD-based fintech · Mexico & Nepal · $6,000 won · Functional prototype built
detailedDescription: The problem: 250 million informal workers in Mexico and Nepal operate entirely outside the formal financial system. No credit score. No bank account. No safety net.
What I built: A USSD-based fintech platform requiring zero smartphone or internet access. Built the functional prototype, defined the technical architecture, and led investor pitches.
How I validated it: Conducted TAM/SAM/SOM analysis across two emerging markets. Won $6,000 across business competitions. Refined unit economics through three rounds of judge feedback.
What I'd do differently: I'd invest earlier in agent network partnerships before building the technology. Distribution is harder than the product in emerging markets.
projects.why: I grew up seeing how hard it is to access reliable financial tools outside traditional banking. Ruta'al exists to remove friction for unbanked workers using channels they already trust (USSD) and a product-first approach to credibility and adoption.
results: Validated demand across Nepal and Mexico through early interviews and prototype demos
projects.doDifferently: Invest earlier in agent network partnerships before building the technology. Distribution is harder than the product in emerging markets; I learned this late.

projects.name: Vigil — Geopolitical Intelligence Dashboard
projects.oneLiner: Live geopolitical intelligence dashboard for traders and risk teams using 9 data sources and AI analysis.
projects.description: Vigil monitors global geopolitical threats and surfaces their financial market implications in real time. It pulls from 9 data sources, runs a 3-phase scoring pipeline, and ranks deduplicated threats alongside portfolio exposure insights. Built with Next.js, TypeScript, and Convex; deployed on Vercel.
projects.projectContext: Next.js 16 · TypeScript · React 18 · Convex · Vitest · CSS Modules · Vercel · Gemini AI
detailedDescription: The problem: Traders and risk teams often see fragmented geopolitical signals across markets, macro data, and event feeds, making it hard to prioritize real risk in real time.
What I built: A Bloomberg Terminal-style dashboard with a live threat feed, sector risk heatmap, portfolio exposure tracker, and force decomposition panel updating every 15 seconds.
Scoring + data pipeline: Built a 3-phase threat scoring system (confidence tiering, probability bias correction, and temporal decay) plus Jaccard-based deduplication across 9 sources.
AI + reliability: Integrated Gemini for on-demand threat analysis with endpoint-level rate limiting, graceful fallback behavior, and Convex tracking for post-hoc directional accuracy.
projects.why: I wanted to unify geopolitical signal detection and market impact analysis in one system, so decision-makers can act on ranked risk instead of chasing disconnected headlines.
results: Aggregated 9 live sources in parallel with graceful degradation when providers fail
Designed 3-phase scoring with probability bias correction and temporal decay
Implemented Jaccard similarity deduplication to merge correlated threats
Added regime-switching sensitivity when global risk crosses escalation thresholds
Maintained TypeScript strict mode and full test coverage across scoring and pipeline logic
projects.doDifferently: I would add a replay mode sooner so teams can backtest alert quality against market moves over longer windows and tune thresholds faster.

projects.name: CardSense: AI Credit Card Optimizer
projects.description: Full-stack web app built with React, Cursor, and Gemini API. Analyzes transaction data to identify optimal card rewards and calculate annual opportunity costs across 15+ products. Deployed on Vercel.
projects.projectContext: React · Gemini API · Deployed on Vercel
detailedDescription: The problem: Most people use one or two credit cards out of habit, leaving hundreds of dollars in annual rewards on the table.
What I built: Full-stack React app using Gemini API to analyze transaction patterns and calculate opportunity cost across 15+ card products.
Key product decision: Designed the output as an "annual opportunity cost" figure rather than a reward comparison; framing it as money lost rather than money gained tested better with users.
Status: Live on Vercel. View CardSense
projects.why: I’m fascinated by consumer fintech and incentives. CardSense exists to turn messy spending data into clear, actionable decisions: what card to use, what you’re leaving on the table, and how to optimize rewards without spreadsheets.

projects.name: VibeTicker: Market Sentiment Dashboard
projects.description: Real-time dashboard comparing AI-analyzed financial news sentiment with live equity, ETF, and crypto price data. Built with TypeScript and server-side API integration using Gemini.
projects.projectContext: TypeScript · Gemini API · Real-time sentiment vs. price data
detailedDescription: The problem: Retail investors react to news headlines without any structured way to correlate sentiment with actual price movement in real time.
What I built: A TypeScript dashboard that pulls live Gemini-analyzed news sentiment and maps it against equity, ETF, and crypto price data via server-side API integration.
Technical decision I made: Chose server-side API calls over client-side to avoid exposing API keys and reduce latency, a tradeoff between security and build complexity.
Status: Live on Vercel. View VibeTicker
projects.why: This started from my obsession with markets: I wanted a single lens that connects narrative (news) to price action in real time, so you can test whether sentiment actually leads returns or just feels convincing.

projects.name: Common Table: Campus Food Shelf Resources
projects.oneLiner: Hackathon app for nearby food resources
projects.description: Built a weekly-view resource finder for the St. Thomas community showing food shelves within a 15-mile radius. Includes calendar-based discovery and a map view for fast navigation.
projects.why: Built at Tommie Buildfest to make it easier to find nearby food shelf resources when the campus pantry is closed, optimized for speed, clarity, and real-world constraints.
results: Shipped a working demo during the hackathon
Designed for quick recruiter-style scanning (calendar + map)

contact.available: May 2026 · OPT eligible · ~3 years no employer action required
